steps.title: Find the growth rate p of f(x)
steps.content: For $f(x) = k(1.72)^x$, increasing $x$ by 1 multiplies the output by 1.72: $$f(x+1) = k(1.72)^{x+1} = 1.72 \cdot k(1.72)^x = 1.72 \cdot f(x).$$ So $f(x)$ increases by $p = 72\%$ for every unit increase in $x$.

steps.title: Use the required four-unit growth
steps.content: For $g$, increasing $x$ by $4$ should multiply the output by $1.72$, the same growth factor that $f$ gets from a one-unit increase.

steps.title: Pick the matching ratio
steps.content:
Choice A — $g(x) = k (1.72^x)^{\frac{1}{4}}$ gives $1.72$ exactly:
$$g(x)=k(1.72^x)^{1/4}=k(1.72)^{x/4}$$
When $x$ increases by $4$, the exponent increases by $1$, so the output is multiplied by $1.72$. The formulas $g(x) = k (1.72^x)^4$, $g(x) = k (1.72)^{x-4}$, and $g(x) = k (1.72)^{x+4}$ multiply by powers other than $1.72$. Choice A — $g(x) = k (1.72^x)^{\frac{1}{4}}$.
